private_memo:
# Morning memo — 2026-06-07

**Source failures:** None observed during fetch. The approved browse tool read the promoted Cloudflare, Simon Willison, GitHub, and rsync-analysis pages successfully enough for scoring.

## Worth attention

- **OpenAI Lockdown Mode** — https://simonwillison.net/2026/Jun/5/openai-help-lockdown-mode/#atom-everything
  OpenAI has now rolled out Lockdown Mode to eligible personal ChatGPT accounts and self-serve Business accounts. The important part is not the branding; it specifically limits outbound network requests that could exfiltrate sensitive data during prompt-injection attacks, which is a concrete mitigation for high-risk users. If you handle sensitive material in ChatGPT, review whether the reduced functionality tradeoff is worth it for your account profile.

- **Cloudflare AI Gateway spend limits** — https://blog.cloudflare.com/ai-gateway-spend-limits/
  Cloudflare added dollar-based spend limits to AI Gateway and is beta-testing identity-driven budgets and routing through Cloudflare Access. That matters because a lot of teams still run shared provider keys with poor attribution and no clean way to stop a runaway agent or CI job before the invoice arrives. If you already proxy model traffic, this is worth evaluating as a budget-control layer; if not, it is still a good reference design for how AI cost controls should work.

- **MicroPython sandbox in WASM** — https://simonwillison.net/2026/Jun/6/micropython-in-a-sandbox/#atom-everything
  Simon Willison published an alpha package that runs Python in a WebAssembly sandbox using MicroPython and wasmtime, with explicit attention to CPU, memory, file, and network controls. The interesting part is not that it is AI-built; it is that it offers a plausible route to safer plugin or agent execution without inventing a whole separate infra stack. This is still alpha, so the immediate move is evaluation rather than adoption, but it is one of the more concrete sandboxing directions to watch.

- **pg_durable brings workflows inside Postgres** — https://github.com/microsoft/pg_durable
  Microsoft’s pg_durable tries to collapse background-work plumbing into a Postgres extension: define workflow steps in SQL, checkpoint them in-database, and resume after failure without an external orchestrator. That will not replace Temporal-style systems everywhere, but it is a real architectural option for teams whose workflows already live next to Postgres data and whose current setup is mostly cron jobs, queues, and status tables. Worth attention if you keep feeling orchestration gravity pull back toward the database.

- **The rsync/Claude bug claim finally got a real analysis** — https://alexispurslane.github.io/rsync-analysis/
  The useful part of this post is methodological discipline: instead of repeating the vibe-coded outrage cycle, it assembles release-level bug data and reports that the recent Claude-assisted rsync releases do not look statistically unusual by the chosen metrics. That does not prove AI coding is safe in general, but it does raise the bar on a widely repeated claim that had been traveling mostly on anecdote and anger. The move here is not to treat the conclusion as final truth; it is to treat this as the minimum evidence standard for future AI-caused-regressions arguments.

## Full digest
